summary: 18th-century Bristol poet who forged medieval poems attributed to the monk Thomas Rowley. Committed suicide at age 17, becoming a Romantic icon. Called a "marvellous Boy" by Wordsworth; dedicatee of Keats's Endymion.

works.name: Thomas Rowley Forgeries
works.description: Chatterton passed off his own poems as the work of the fictional 15th-century monk Thomas Rowley.

clues.clue: Forged poems attributed to the 15th-century monk Thomas Rowley
examples: tried to pass off his work as that of the 15th-century monk Thomas Rowley
passed off a bunch of faux-medieval writings as works of the monk 'Thomas Rowley'

works.name: Suicide and Romantic Legacy
works.description: Died by suicide at age 17 (arsenic poisoning). Henry Wallis painted his death scene (model was George Meredith). A major icon for the Romantics.

comprehensive_summary: Thomas Chatterton is clued as an 18th-century Bristol poet who forged medieval poems attributed to the fictional 15th-century monk Thomas Rowley. He committed suicide at age 17, reportedly by arsenic poisoning, and became a major icon for the Romantics. His death scene was painted by Henry Wallis using George Meredith as a model; the painting hangs in the Tate. Peter Ackroyd wrote a novel about him. Wordsworth called him a "marvellous Boy" in "Resolution and Independence," and Keats dedicated Endymion to him. Shelley names him alongside Sidney and Lucan as "inheritors of unfulfill'd renown" in Adonais. Dante Gabriel Rossetti attributed "Shakespeare's manhood at a boy's wild heart" to Chatterton. His forged works include Aella, a Tragical Interlude, "An Excellent Ballad of Charitie," and "The Parliament of Sprites." Before leaving Bristol, he wrote a satirical "Last Will and Testament." Robert Browning adopted the term "slug-horn" from Chatterton's pseudo-medieval language.
